title: Quick check: closing the loop in the ticketing system

choices: Closing the Huntress incident and walking away — "closed in Huntress, forgot the PSA." The Huntress side feels like the real work; the PSA close feels like paperwork. Both are required.

choices.label: Approving a remediation without reading the Evidence.
choices.rationale: Different failure; not this lesson's trap.

choices.label: Calling the customer before isolating the host.
choices.rationale: Different lesson's failure.

choices.label: Documenting the action in the PSA without copying the note to Huntress.
choices.rationale: Inverted direction; the named trap is the other way around.

questions.explanation: Both systems hold the work. Closing one without the other is the drift trap named in the helpdesk-traps lesson.

questions.prompt: Put the six close-out steps in order.
items: Close the Huntress incident with the right resolution disposition.
Match the PSA ticket — find the corresponding ticket.
Add the incident note to the PSA ticket.
Enter time per the actual time spent.
Set the category and resolution code on the PSA.
questions.explanation: Six small steps, takes two minutes, prevents the drift trap.

questions.prompt: Why does PSA closure matter at all if Huntress already records the security action?
choices: The PSA holds the time, the billing, and the customer-facing record. Auditors don't always have access to Huntress; the PSA is usually the canonical record.

choices.label: Huntress automatically purges incident data after 30 days; the PSA is the archive.
choices.rationale: Not the reason. The reason is auditing, billing, and customer-record.

choices.label: The PSA is the only system the SOC can see.
choices.rationale: Inverts the reality. The SOC works in Huntress; the PSA is the MSP-side record.

choices.label: Without PSA closure, the Huntress incident can't be closed either.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the mechanism. Huntress can close independently.

questions.explanation: PSA is the canonical MSP-side record. Auditors and billing reads from the PSA, not from Huntress.

questions.prompt: A tech enters generic time and category on a PSA close-out to "save time." What does the lesson say happens?
choices: Five seconds saved at close becomes ten minutes of explanation at month-end when the report makes no sense. Categorise specifically.

choices.label: Nothing — the PSA's reports aggregate any category.
choices.rationale: Generic categories produce noisy reports.

choices.label: The PSA auto-corrects generic categories based on integration metadata.
choices.rationale: Not a feature.

choices.label: Saving time on categories is the lesson's recommended practice for Lows.
choices.rationale: Inverts the rule entirely.

questions.explanation: Specific category, actual time. The five seconds you save at close cost ten minutes at month-end.

questions.prompt: End of shift, 16:55. Three Huntress incidents in "closed" state but PSA tickets still open. Two have notes; one doesn't. You finish at 17:00. What do you do?

choices.label: Close the three PSA tickets quickly with generic time entry and category; leave the missing note for tomorrow.
choices.rationale: Generic time and category creates reporting noise; leaving the missing note for tomorrow means it never gets written (memory degrades overnight).

choices: Spend the five minutes to close all three correctly — actual time per ticket, specific category, note copied for the one missing.

choices.label: Leave them all for tomorrow because the shift is ending.
choices.rationale: Tomorrow's queue starts with three half-done items from yesterday on top of new work.

questions.explanation: Five minutes now versus tomorrow's reconstruction. End-of-shift discipline keeps tomorrow's queue clean.

questions.prompt: You find a PSA ticket open for a week with no Huntress incident matching it. The PSA description says "EDR incident on WS-EXAMPLE-FIELD-12" and references an incident ID that doesn't exist in Huntress. What do you do?

choices.label: Close the PSA ticket as "unable to locate."
choices.rationale: Closing without investigating could hide real work that needed doing.

choices: Open the PSA fully. Read who created it, when, why. Search Huntress for the host's recent incidents, not just the referenced ID. Reconcile based on what you find, then close cleanly.

choices.label: Bump to senior immediately.
choices.rationale: Defensible but premature. The investigation is in scope for a tech; senior would ask what you already found.

questions.explanation: The integration may have closed an old incident with a different ID; the typed reference may have a typo. Investigate first.

questions.prompt: The PSA ticket for the incident doesn't exist and no integration is in place to auto-create one. What's the recommendation?
choices: Could be a one-off integration failure or a customer with different PSA mapping. Bump rather than create-from-scratch on customers you don't know the conventions for.

choices.label: Create a PSA ticket from scratch using your best guess.
choices.rationale: Creates noise if your guess differs from the customer's PSA structure.

choices.label: Skip the PSA close and document only in Huntress.
choices.rationale: Defeats the close-the-loop discipline.

choices.label: Email Huntress support to ask them to create the PSA ticket.
choices.rationale: Misuses support; PSA mapping isn't a Huntress-support question.

questions.explanation: Don't invent PSA structure for customers you don't know. Bump.

questions.prompt: You spot drift between a closed Huntress incident and an open PSA ticket from three weeks ago. What's the move?
choices: Drift gets cleaned up when you find it, not "next time." A drifted ticket from three weeks ago is harder to reconcile than one from three minutes ago.

choices.label: Old drift is fine to leave; the customer report is already out.
choices.rationale: Old drift means worse reconstruction work and worse audit trail.

choices.label: Close the PSA without a note; Huntress has the detail.
choices.rationale: Loses the canonical PSA-side record.

choices.label: Email the customer to apologise for the stale ticket.
choices.rationale: Misframes drift as a customer-relationship issue; it's an internal-record one.

questions.explanation: Reconcile when found. Cost grows with age.

questions.prompt: The time-entry category needed isn't in the PSA's available list. What does the lesson say?
choices: Could be the PSA has been restructured and the runbook is out of date. Bump.

choices.label: Use any close-enough category; categories don't matter long-term.
choices.rationale: Generic / wrong categories create downstream reporting problems.

choices.label: Create a new category in the PSA on the fly.
choices.rationale: Inventing categories without coordination creates a reporting taxonomy mess.

choices.label: Leave the category blank and close the ticket.
choices.rationale: Blank category compounds the reporting problem.

questions.explanation: Missing category is a runbook-versus-PSA mismatch worth a senior look.

questions.prompt: Two PSA tickets existing for the same Huntress incident usually means the tech opened one manually while the integration also opened one — merge or close the duplicate.
questions.explanation: Manual-plus-integration creation collision. The lesson's named drift shape.
